title: Essential Hypertension

patient.name: James Wilson
patient.persona: busy executive, dismissive of health concerns

chief_complaint: I came for a routine check-up. I feel fine. My blood pressure was high at a work health fair last week.

physical_exam.Fundoscopic: Arteriolar narrowing, no hemorrhages or papilledema
physical_exam.Cardiac: Regular rhythm, S4 gallop present, PMI sustained

hidden_data.ecg: Sinus rhythm, left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) pattern - Sokolow-Lyon criteria positive
hidden_data.echo: Left ventricular hypertrophy, EF 60%, diastolic dysfunction grade 1

correct_diagnosis: Essential Hypertension Stage 2 with Left Ventricular Hypertrophy
differential_diagnoses: Secondary Hypertension (Renal artery stenosis, Primary aldosteronism, Pheochromocytoma)
key_learning_points: Hypertension is diagnosed when BP ≥130/80 (ACC/AHA 2017) on 2 separate occasions
Stage 2 HTN: SBP ≥140 or DBP ≥90 - requires pharmacologic therapy in addition to lifestyle modifications
LVH on ECG or Echo is evidence of end-organ damage and indicates long-standing uncontrolled HTN
First-line agents: ACEi/ARB, CCB, or thiazide diuretic. Beta-blockers are no longer first-line for uncomplicated HTN
Secondary causes should be considered in young patients (<30), resistant HTN, or sudden onset

patient_responses.lifestyle: I work 60 hours a week. I eat out a lot, don't exercise. I drink 3-4 cups of coffee and maybe 2-3 beers on weekends.
patient_responses.family history: My mother has high blood pressure. My father had a stroke at 68.
patient_responses.symptoms: I honestly feel fine. No headaches, no vision problems. Maybe a little more tired than usual.
patient_responses.medications: None regularly. I take ibuprofen for occasional back pain.

hints.history: Screen for symptoms of secondary HTN: headaches, palpitations, sweating (pheochromocytoma), muscle weakness (hyperaldosteronism)
hints.examination: Fundoscopic exam for retinopathy, cardiac exam for LVH, abdominal bruits for renal artery stenosis
hints.labs: CMP, lipid panel, HbA1c, urinalysis for proteinuria. TSH, renal ultrasound if suspecting secondary causes.
hints.imaging: Echo to evaluate for LVH. ECG shows LVH pattern. Ambulatory BP monitoring if white coat HTN suspected.

feedback.correct_message: Well done! You identified Stage 2 essential hypertension with LVH - evidence of end-organ damage. This requires aggressive lifestyle modification and pharmacologic therapy to reduce cardiovascular risk.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good thinking, but this is essential hypertension with end-organ damage, not just elevated BP numbers. The LVH on ECG indicates long-standing uncontrolled HTN requiring treatment.
feedback.incorrect_message: This patient has Stage 2 essential hypertension with LVH on ECG. The presence of end-organ damage makes this more urgent - lifestyle changes alone are insufficient. Pharmacologic therapy is indicated.
clinical_pearls: S4 gallop suggests decreased LV compliance from hypertrophy - a sign of chronic HTN
First-line anti-hypertensives: ACEi/ARB, CCB, or thiazide. Choose based on comorbidities
ACEi/ARB are preferred in patients with LVH as they promote LVH regression

ecg.comment: Identifies LVH pattern and old MI. Critical for risk stratification

echo.comment: Confirms LVH, assesses EF, and evaluates diastolic function

cmp.comment: Evaluates renal function and potassium before starting ACEi/ARB

hba1c.comment: Screens for diabetes - common comorbidity with HTN

lipid_panel.comment: Guides statin therapy initiation. Most HTN patients benefit from statin

missing_tests: Urine microalbumin - Early marker of hypertensive nephropathy
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, excellent workup. Start Amlodipine 5mg daily + Lisinopril 10mg daily (ACEi for LVH regression). Advise DASH diet, sodium <2g/day, exercise 150min/week, and limit alcohol. Follow-up BP in 4 weeks.
